item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I haven't, but I have a passing familiarity with Cortázar and I will admit that magic realism is a strong influence in my writing.

Basically, I had the idea of a permanent traffic jam for a long time, but couldn't think of an angle why a traffic jam was important. Then, I had an opportunity to move to another country, and had all those thoughts: what about my job? What about my home? And then, am I really going to let those attachments stop me? And that was the angle.

I eventually didn't take the opportunity, but that was because I recognized it wasn't going to actually be a good time.

Incidentally, magic realism is an excellent source of SCP fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have you ever read The Southern Thruway (or "La autopista del sur" in its original Spanish) written by Julio Cortazar? Because this skip is so similar to its main points as to seem straight out plagiarized and formatted to fit the site. I'm actually surprised no one has mentioned that until now. I guess Julio Cortazar is not as well known to the English speaking world as I thought.

I'm not accusing you or anything, I just want to know if you came by the idea completely on your own or if you were inspired by the tale. I'm really curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I really like how this one in particular came out, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Darn, WJS has written all of the good stuff on the site.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for sure.

This is fantastic, with its creeping sense of how skewed the affected people have become, how irrational their beliefs are while at the same time being rooted in a very rational thought process - do not jump the queue, be polite, work your way up the pecking order. It's suitably ridiculous but at the same time you don't lose sight of the fact that people are ruining their lives for this microcosm. Very effective!

I feel like maybe this SCP exists in minor forms in railway stations around the UK…

item.title: Image Update
item.content:encoded:
I was looking through my files and realized that I didn't have the CC evidence on the picture originally on this article. (A lot of people had used the photo in other works around the world, but its true original provenance and license got really muddled.) I have updated it to one that has most definitely the CC license, taken by Lingaraj GJ aka N-O-M-A-D.

Yes, this is in Delhi, not Karachi, but you make do with what you can find.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems like that would just make the people inside upset? The taxi passenger shows there's no end to this madness.

This is one of my favorites. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …So did they consider just setting up a giant tow cable and hauling entire cars from the area?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait, I didn't upvote this?

For me, this is one of the best articles on the site, because it's a clever commentary on the foundations of societies as well as the status of the one we're in right now, being chillingly realistic all the while (except for the part where there's an anomaly but that doesn't count). Basically, it may or may not appeal to people who aren't sociology nerds, but for those who are, I can't think of anything that does it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm wishing there was an Economic tag. Of course, that's what my PhD is in, so I suppose I'm biased.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. It's unsettling without being gruesome, and the reactions are really interesting. For instance, based on the interview logs, I'm now wondering what would happen if you ignored the people and just started targeting private property and retrieving it from the zone. If someone justifies their refusal to leave the area by deciding they can't abandon their stuff, how would they react if all of that stuff was suddenly outside the area? Especially if said stuff was still plainly in view with unobstructed access, so that they could easily have it back by just walking toward it? And if they did come up with a reason why they still couldn't leave, how would they justify the change in their reasoning? Fascinating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting bit +1 from me

I don't like the final implication on the "die for" belief thing, but damn if the whole read wasn't interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me this SCP did just fine as it is. In fact, I loved it.
I've read some number of SCPs now, and I came to prefer a simple one over the ones with complicated background.
And this is the article that builds up on a very simple premise and managed to create certain feelings of climax. Upvoted with no hesitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The sunk costs fallacy turned up to 11. I like it :). +1

I also like the way the anomaly causes them to think they can't leave, but then they develop their own internalized reasons for it, and convince themselves it's a (rational) choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's part of my "Disorient the reader during the Special Containment Procedures" tactic. You're right, that does sound horrible. But if you're one of the personnel affected by the anomaly, then being told you'll be kept in place is a relief.

I didn't get this at all before reading your response to @psul — instead, it came across as the clichéd joke "the (beatings/firings) will continue until morale improves"

Perhaps it could be made to better convey what you say you were trying to say?

"Furthermore, if affected agents they show signs of decreased morale due to being repeatedly pulled out of the anomalous area, they are to be reminded reassured that they will remain in place until the traffic clears"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's… okay? A little drawn out, not a thoroughly entertaining read. I believe the way the story is presented tries to show the events happening chronologically, but to me it makes the plot roll out too slowly. The formation of a unique society was interesting to me but warranted more of a short block in a larger story in my opinion than an entire article. No-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reorganizing and recontexualizing a number of bits here has improved this, and brought it into upvote territory, even if I'm still not completely sold on the interviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All right, I've been waiting for this one since you mentioned it in on the Fill Up the SCP Map Challenge thread! Superbly written, and I really like the idea of whole communities forming around a pop-up anomaly. I was actually considering doing something similar a while ago, albeit based on a massive parking lot… but this is better than anything I could have come up with. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Collapsed for length
- Hide This

how many people were originally caught in the effect? When I read "four intersections", I imagined no more than around 200 vehicles. However removing 50 people from that environment would be a large percentage of the total - perhaps it's worth noting somewhere the approximate number originally subject to the anomaly?

I never considered an exact number, but I figured there would be over 1,000 people in the effect, including passengers. Removing 50 is significant, but the problems are still fully evident.

"Furthermore, if they show signs of decreased morale, they are to be reminded that they will remain in place until the traffic clears." - is that the Foundation threatening their own personnel? "Too bad if you're unhappy - you're stuck there"?

That's part of my "Disorient the reader during the Special Containment Procedures" tactic. You're right, that does sound horrible. But if you're one of the personnel affected by the anomaly, then being told you'll be kept in place is a relief.

"and to log any behavior changes found" - I think "behavioral" would be marginally more correct.

I fix.

"Provincial forces disobeyed orders, despite refusing to back down from the task of clearing the traffic." - it's not clear from this what orders were being disobeyed. Does this cover what you mean: "Provincial forces disobeyed orders to leave the area, refusing to back down from the task of clearing the traffic."?

Yes, I'll make that distinction.

"Although civilians would leave their cars and walking around, no one left the roadway at any time." - tense issue: "would leave their cars and walk around" or "begin walking around".

Thanks, I'll fix that.

"MTF Phi-12 contacted civilians, requesting medical evacuation. All requests were refused." - it sounds like Phi-12 was asking the civilians to help the MTF leave the area. "Offering" and "all offers were refused" might be clearer.

Agreed, will fix.

Not a big deal, but Agent Shah sounds quite bewildered and frustrated in the final interview. That doesn't quite make sense to me - Agent Shah has been studying the anomaly for at least 3 months, so would presumably not be surprised by these answers.

That's part of Protocol Balakot-525. Use every opportunity to indicate the absurdity of the position of staying. Yes, Agent Shah has heard it all before. He still hammers on that it's ludicrous to stay in traffic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Loved this. Fun to read, engaging, good symbolism on society/situations, and still a little creepy.

EDIT: Still thinking about this one, hours later. You know that's the mark of a great skip. I was thinking about how mundane and simple it really is. It's just an aura that makes you want to stay. And yet the execution, the cognitive dissonance, the suspension of belief that the victims have, everything about the skip was just carried out so well. I think this is a classic example of a simple idea executed perfectly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I no-voted the first version, but have upvoted this one. The reorganisation of material makes for easier reading, and the new description is not only clearer and more interesting, but the tone feels better as well.

There are still a few things I don't understand:

  • how many people were originally caught in the effect? When I read "four intersections", I imagined no more than around 200 vehicles. However removing 50 people from that environment would be a large percentage of the total - perhaps it's worth noting somewhere the approximate number originally subject to the anomaly?
  • "Furthermore, if they show signs of decreased morale, they are to be reminded that they will remain in place until the traffic clears." - is that the Foundation threatening their own personnel? "Too bad if you're unhappy - you're stuck there"?
  • "and to log any behavior changes found" - I think "behavioral" would be marginally more correct.
  • "Provincial forces disobeyed orders, despite refusing to back down from the task of clearing the traffic." - it's not clear from this what orders were being disobeyed. Does this cover what you mean: "Provincial forces disobeyed orders to leave the area, refusing to back down from the task of clearing the traffic."?
  • "Although civilians would leave their cars and walking around, no one left the roadway at any time." - tense issue: "would leave their cars and walk around" or "begin walking around".
  • "MTF Phi-12 contacted civilians, requesting medical evacuation. All requests were refused." - it sounds like Phi-12 was asking the civilians to help the MTF leave the area. "Offering" and "all offers were refused" might be clearer.
  • Not a big deal, but Agent Shah sounds quite bewildered and frustrated in the final interview. That doesn't quite make sense to me - Agent Shah has been studying the anomaly for at least 3 months, so would presumably not be surprised by these answers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And vote reversed, hurray!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. The last line of the interview nailed it home for me. The belief that belief is inherently valuable enough to die for just because you are experiencing believing. So good. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big update.

  • Explained the structure of the cognitohazard better.
  • Cleaned up the chronology for reading.
  • Feathered the interviews into the chronology.

Let me know if you like it better, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this, and I can't quite explain why. My only criticism (easily fixed) is that there seems to be something odd going on with the tenses/writing style in parts, e.g.

A small riot broke out within the zone on 20 May 2015, which was quickly suppressed by affected police. The cause of the riot was a disagreement regarding the dispensation of the rescued civilian's vehicle. Affected individuals meet and debate the situation, agreeing that vehicles and equipment of rescued vehicles are considered abandoned.

Seeing as no-one else has mentioned it I'm wondering whether it's deliberate and I'm missing something, but I found it a little distracting. +1 anyway because I think the idea is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It is a weird little take on pretty much any form of society/civilization as a whole. Actually, seen through that lens I could imagine this an an AWCY installation. Or at least, something that makes every anartist who discovers it go "Why didn't I think of that?"

A few questions that came to mind is what would happen if you nabbed a person's car (steel cable/winch to drag it out) and petrol (somehow) along with them. Presumably, the obsession would shift to the time they had lost getting where they wanted to go/their social standing inside the area.
What would happen if you started clearing the traffic jam that way?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like these people had their perception of value invested turned up to an irrational level. Like they lose the ability to to weigh net gains and losses through their actions and ignore any possible losses as a result of attempting to prevent a loss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the mechanics of the anomaly aren't completely described as I elaborated above. Although this is, I think, an easy fix.

How the society develops is certainly interesting, but I think it would be better as a supplement or even a tale (probably in first person from the POV of a person who was there near the beginning).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on advice, I'm going to clean up the article some, but the big thing I'm curious about is how many people feel there's something more, some sort of commentary, that isn't complete. Although, yes, there is allegory in this, I didn't want to hit people over the head with it.

On the other hand, I could easily expand this further, more descriptions of deteriorating conditions, more debates on what's going on and how best to rescue and rehabilitate civilians… essentially a chronology instead of exploration logs, describing how the society in the traffic jam develops. But would that bog down the SCP article? This situation, I could pull a 20-page short story out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tentative upvote from me. I would like to see more done with this. Especially when you put people in a crammed space with no facilities or sustenance. People would be dying within a week.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I'm overstepping, but...
...No, I'm not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Locational cognitive hazard?

"SCP-2459 is a cognitive hazard affecting an area of four intersections within Bin Qasim, Pakistan. Any person who enters the zone of effect spontaneously generates a complex memetic system representing a consistent set of values which then are refined into a consensus via social contact. Affected persons are convinced that leaving the zone is not an option, typically for reasons relating to social standing and protection of property rights. The primary result of this hazard is a large traffic jam that extends throughout the city blocks in the zone of effect."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe you can help me, then.

  • It's not propagated by signage.
  • It's not propagated by knowledge of your location.
  • It's not propagated by language.

However,

  • It is propagated by location.
  • It is propagated by societal engagement.
  • It facilitates a consensus agreement about values.

What would you call it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2459 is a memetic hazard

As I said it is either incorrectly or incompletely described. If it is not a memetic hazard, the quoted statement is incorrect. If it is a memetic hazard, there is a vector and therefore hard countermeasures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't have to be memetic. That's not necessarily how it works, or how it has to work. It's a phenomenon that just happens because it does and because it can, and it's not bound by specific rules, even anomalous rules. That's why it's so strange and so weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That doesn't make any sense though, being in an area has no inherent memetic content. Location isn't information. Information about a location is, but merely "You are at such and such a place" seems to be too sparse a message to be a carrier for "and you can't leave it without loss of personal property or social status".

Even if that is the carrier, why don't you just not tell the rescue team where they are (a blind drop) and instruct them to avoid reading locational signage or discussing location with the locals? Sending rescuers who aren't fluent in Urdu would avoid all vectors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless it does happen again, there's nothing to figure out yet, any more than we're likely to figure out where SCP-270 came from or how the sound stage in SCP-024 came to host the Game Show of Death. All we can do at this point is try to figure out how to rescue the civilians caught here, and hope that if the number drops below a certain point the anomaly will cease to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very odd.

I half expected it to be a -J, some kind of commentary about economics or such. But it feels like there's something missing and the article doesn't feel complete at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The vector of infection is just being in the area. That's all there is. I thought that was explained simply enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think a possible improvement could come from putting the days as separate events, bolded perhaps. It might make it easier to read.

As for the story and such, I enjoyed it, and it gets a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it is memetic and the vector is talking to affected persons why don't rescuers just wear earplugs and avoid conversation?

I feel like the mechanics of the effect need to be explained better.

ETA: On reflection, I feel I need to give this my most reluctant downvote yet. I really like this, but I find that the effect is either too incompletely or too incorrectly described and that the Foundation would definitely want to identify the source better and deploy countermeasures. This hole is so conspicuous that I can't ignore it, despite the overall excellence of this article. :(

item.title: Re: Social commentary?
item.content:encoded: Yeah, no reflections on the Middle East. Or South Asia, really. I originally had placed this in China, but as Myriad points out below, this could have happened anywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cognitohazard or memetic? It was a real gray area for me. Certainly, the initial disorientation that happens when someone steps onto the roadway is a locational cognitohazard. However, the society that the road people developed is memetic in nature, and ends up supporting the conclusion that it's best not to leave until the traffic clears. I could spend more time dissecting the nature of the anomaly into its cognitohazardous and memetic components, but I thought that would get in the way of the story. I did ask in chat, is this cognitohazardous or memetic? More people said memetic than cognitohazardous, but yes, it's not cut and dried one or the other.

The Pakistanis I know don't say "God willing" with any frequency, so I thought it wouldn't be correct. I'll admit I'm less familiar with Sindhi language markers, so I thought it best not to guess.

The black boxes originally were for New Ordos, as an earlier draft placed the anomaly in China. Originally, this was why New Ordos is empty; it's not that no one moved in, it's that they're all stuck in traffic. But, yes, this was moved to Sindh Province in Pakistan for MrWrong's map challenge, so let's say it's in the town of Bin Qasim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I feel like the article is missing a chunk. This is about an effect targeted specifically at a very high population area that just showed up one day.

There should be at least something dealing with trying to figure out why it happened, whether it's an isolated incident, and how to keep it from happening it again if it might not be an isolated incident. I mean, Karachi (it doesn't fit the name I know, but that's where the pic is from) is bad enough, but what happens if this shows up in New York or Shanghai?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the development of the piece, since most discovery process for SCPs warrant a simple mention. In this case, it is developed as a story element. Furthermore, I enjoy the weirdness of it and fairly creeped out by it.

Overall, an upvote is deserved and I acknowledge you to have passed my challenge.

item.title: Re: Social commentary?
item.content:encoded: You do know that Pakistan is in South Asia…

item.title: Social commentary?
item.content:encoded: Petrol, society, a deadlock situation, reflections on middle east?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is the vector of infection? Why not just block that? This is what's keeping me from upvoting, right now it is reading an awful lot like a memetic "aura". :(

Also, do Urdu speakers not have a "God willing", like Arabic Muslims? Or do they not use it as frequently as Arabic speakers say inshalla?

The blackboxes don't seem to cover any city in Pakistan that is large enough to have this much traffic, but I could be wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have some thoughts on how to organize the chronology more prettily, but I'm going to sleep on it. Hopefully that will help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This gets exhausting for me to read, majorly.

I'm one of those people who can't focus on reading if there's no shifts in the way text is presented, and lose my place fairly quickly. As it is, from the description to the interview (more specifically, from the history of the anomaly to the interview), it reads as one giant wall of text and is unappealing and hard for me to get through. I dunno what you'd wanna do to make it not just look like words, but something!1

Other notes: this seems like it'd be Keter, because the problem wouldn't just be unpredictable if you leave it alone. That's not to say it'd "break out of containment", but you have a mass group of individuals living in this cramped and tight space full of vehicles, people of all sorts, and an anomaly who's range is not only fairly big, but also unpredictable in of itself. It might just decide to grow on its own in the future, and there's no concrete way to make sure that's not the case. It might be an edge case between Euclid and Keter, but it really seems Keter to me.

The writing style changes starting at "MTF Phi-4 completed securing perimeter on 14 May 2015." which reads weirdly when you transition from full sentences to this, in the same section no less. If you wanna keep it in the description, try changing the writing. Or put it in a new section like this.

I don't know how much the interviews add. The second one is more interesting than the first, and the third one already states stuff that the first one hints at. However, they weren't interesting until the third one, where some of the logic unfolded but it was played fairly straight for the absurdity it tried to present.

Footnotes
1. This opinion is most likely going to be in the minority.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck? Is this why traffic is terrible in big cities? Is this what you people in Not-New Mexico do when you're on the road?

Seriously, traffic jams are a foreign concept to me. I upvoted this because, to me, this is on 1193 and 2211's level of weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My take on the situations we all get stuck in.

Thanks to A Random Day, kola, Snarky Potato, and TwistedGears for their review in chat!
